skill.name: chatgpt-app-builder
skill.description: Deprecated guidance for building ChatGPT apps with interactive mcp-use widgets and OpenAI Apps SDK patterns. Use mcp-app-builder instead when it is available.
skill.summary: Deprecated ChatGPT app builder guidance for MCP servers, React widgets, state, CSP, and connector setup.
skill.author: mcp-use

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown examples, JSON eval text, and license text. Confirmed risks are operational: the skill recommends package-manager commands, public tunnel setup, optional no-auth connector configuration, and widget capabilities that can call tools or open URLs. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, real weak cryptography use, or hidden code execution.

medium_findings.title: Public Tunnel and Optional No-Authentication Connector Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The setup guide tells users to expose a local MCP server through a tunnel and then allows Authentication: "No Authentication" for ChatGPT app setup. This is legitimate development guidance, but it can expose side-effecting tools if reused outside controlled testing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The public tunnel and no-auth instructions are explicit in the setup flow. Risk depends on the generated app and environment, so this is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Package Manager and Deployment Commands in Skill Instructions
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill recommends npx, npm, tunnel, login, and deploy commands. These are expected for app scaffolding, but marketplace users should review packages and destination services before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and central to the setup workflow. They are not malicious by themselves, but they create normal supply-chain and deployment risk.

medium_findings.title: Widget APIs Can Trigger Tool Calls and External Navigation
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The reference documents intentional APIs for widgets to call MCP tools and open external URLs. This is core app functionality, but generated apps need explicit auth, validation, and user consent for sensitive actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The APIs are documented as intended capabilities, not hidden behavior. The security impact depends on how downstream apps implement tool permissions and side effects.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Reviewed flagged locations contain Markdown prose, JSON eval scenarios, and license text rather than cryptographic APIs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain documentation or evaluation text. There is no executable crypto code at the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Host Context Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Flagged references describe widget host context such as device type, locale, and time zone. This helps responsive UI behavior and is not OS or network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe browser or host-provided widget context and eval prompts. They do not collect filesystem, process, or network environment data.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and CSP Domains Are Example Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are localhost, example API domains, or documented CSP allowlist examples. They do not send secrets or create hidden outbound requests from the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in user-facing examples and configuration documentation. Network behavior would be in apps created by users, not hidden in this skill.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Limited to Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for sensitive file access. The reviewed environment access uses process.env.MCP_URL for a base URL example and mentions CSP_URLS as a configuration variable. No evidence found of reading .env files or exfiltrating secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited uses are normal configuration references. The static sensitive-file finding is not supported by the actual lines reviewed.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed as Malicious
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The analyzer combined command examples, network examples, and environment configuration into a critical heuristic. Semantic review found documentation for building apps, not obfuscated behavior or credential theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky primitives are visible and explained as app-building instructions. No evidence found of hidden execution, encoded payloads, or unauthorized network destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes setup and deployment commands using npx, npm, tunnel tooling, login, and deploy. Users should inspect package sources and run commands in a controlled workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented. They are expected for this skill but still represent executable operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Network Exposure During Development
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide recommends a tunnel URL for ChatGPT connector testing and permits no-auth setup. This can expose tools if used with sensitive data or production-like credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exposure path is clearly stated in the setup steps. The risk is contextual and depends on whether the local server contains sensitive tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Widget-Initiated Backend Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The docs show widgets calling backend tools and opening external URLs. Apps generated from this guidance should require validation, authorization, and user confirmation for sensitive actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is documented as intended app behavior. It becomes dangerous only when downstream tools perform sensitive actions without controls.

content.user_title: Build ChatGPT Apps with MCP Widgets
content.value_statement: ChatGPT app setup can be hard when tools, widgets, state, and CSP are scattered. This deprecated skill gives structured guidance for mcp-use widget apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides discovery for focused ChatGPT app ideas before implementation.
Explains MCP server tools that return interactive widget responses.
Shows React widget structure with widget metadata and provider usage.
Documents persistent state, follow-up messages, tool calls, and external links.
Covers CSP, metadata, display modes, theme, locale, and responsive layout.
Provides setup, local testing, connector, tunnel, and deployment guidance.
limitations: This skill is deprecated in favor of mcp-app-builder.
It provides guidance and examples, not a complete app generator.
Examples may need updates for current mcp-use and OpenAI Apps SDK versions.
Authentication, data handling, and CSP choices remain the builder responsibility.

use_cases.title: Prototype a ChatGPT Widget App
use_cases.description: Plan a narrow app flow, decide which actions need widgets, and scaffold a local mcp-use project.

use_cases.title: Design Tool and Widget Boundaries
use_cases.description: Separate conversational tools from visual widget flows before writing server handlers and React components.

use_cases.title: Review Apps SDK Integration Details
use_cases.description: Check state, theme, display mode, CSP, metadata, and widget API patterns while building a ChatGPT connector.

prompt_templates.title: Start Discovery
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new ChatGPT app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatgpt-app-builder skill to help me scope a ChatGPT app for [business or product]. Ask one discovery question at a time.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Widgets and Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this app spec: [paste spec], decide which flows need widgets and which should be tools only. Explain the reasoning.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Widget Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer creating server and React files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill references to outline the MCP server handler and React widget for [flow]. Include state and pending behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit App Integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before testing in ChatGPT
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned ChatGPT app for widget state, tool side effects, CSP domains, authentication, and connector setup risks.

output_examples.input: I want a ChatGPT app for booking restaurant reservations.
output_examples.output: A discovery plan with problem, user, pain point, core actions, API needs, and a recommendation for search and reservation flows.

output_examples.input: Should my flight search use one widget or several tools?
output_examples.output: A concise architecture recommendation that keeps browsing and selection in one widget and reserves backend actions for tools.

output_examples.input: How should I configure CSP for a weather widget?
output_examples.output: A security-focused checklist for exact API domains, resource domains, HTTPS use, and avoiding unsafe script settings.

best_practices: Use mcp-app-builder instead when it is available, because this skill is deprecated.
Keep widget state for selections and use backend tools only for real server actions.
Use exact CSP domains, authentication, and consent for any tool with side effects.
anti_patterns: Do not expose a local tunnel with no authentication for sensitive or production-like tools.
Do not port a full application into ChatGPT when one to three focused actions would work better.
Do not create extra tools for data already returned to the widget.

faq.question: Is this skill still recommended?
faq.answer: No. It is deprecated and points users to mcp-app-builder when that skill is available.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build ChatGPT apps that use MCP server tools and interactive React widgets.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance, reference patterns, and setup instructions for the developer.

faq.question: Can widgets call backend tools?
faq.answer: Yes. The references show widget tool calls, but sensitive actions need validation, auth, and user consent.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes external commands, public tunnel guidance, network configuration, and environment configuration examples.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
